# Extracao: transcription_mp4_03-The_Economics_of_Storytelling-_Leveraging_Pre-existing_Skills

- **Fonte:** transcription_mp4_03-The_Economics_of_Storytelling-_Leveraging_Pre-existing_Skills.txt
- **Tipo:** TXT
- **Tamanho:** 0 MB

---

Let's talk about the economics of storytelling. Storytelling is cheap. You had to purchase this course presumably, but that's about it. You don't need to build a building. You don't need to invest in software. You don't need to hire on new personnel. You had to purchase this course and you're going to have to teach some people how to use it, but that's it. It is very inexpensive compared to what you're going to get as a result. And the best thing about it is all of your people, yourself included, you're already storytellers. You're already doing the thing that you're trying to do. You might be doing it poorly. The words that are coming out of your mouth might not be the best that they could be, but everyone in your company, yourself included, are already storytellers. The goal is simply to level you up, to take the thing that you're doing imperfectly and bring it to a more perfect level. And that's going to be fantastic for your business because ideally what you want is to have an army of employees who are going out into the world and telling great stories about your company. When your people are sitting down at dinner with friends and they say, what do you do for a living? You want them to tell a great story about your company. You want great stories told on every elevator ride, every plane ride, every single time one of your employees is out in the world and someone says, what do you do for a living? You want them to tell a great story that is fantastic advertising for you. So simply by leveling your people up, getting them to do the thing that they already know how to do, it's going to make an enormous difference in your business. My favorite story related to the economics of storytelling is this. I had a meeting scheduled with a client, a two hour meeting at two o'clock in the afternoon, and I lost track of time. Honestly, I was playing outside with my son and I just forgot to look at the time. So when I finally took a look at my phone, I realized it was nearly 3.30. I was 90 minutes late for my meeting. So I texted my client and I apologized and he texted back and said, can you get on the phone for the last 30 minutes? And so we had a zoom call for 30 minutes where we talked about storytelling. At the end of that call, I said, listen, I'm not charging you for that 30 minutes. I really blew it today. I apologize. And he said, no. He said, Matt, I want you to charge me for the full two hours. I said, there's no way I'm charging you for time that I did not put in. And he said, no, listen to me. He said that the 30 minutes that we spent talking about storytelling was the most valuable 30 minutes of his week. And it's not because I'm special. It's not because my wisdom is overwhelming. It's not because I am a brilliant teacher. It's because my client understands that storytelling, sending a finely polished, perfectly carved, highly effective message out into the world is the best thing he can do for his business. And so he insisted on paying me for those two hours. We negotiated. I eventually agreed on a one hour payment for the half hour of work that I did, but I never sent him an invoice and he has yet to notice. But he understands the economics of storytelling. He understands that 30 minutes spent thinking about how the world perceives his business and what he can say and do to change that perception can mean everything for your company. By getting people to understand your mission, your product, your service, your people, that's everything when it comes to trying to do business with people. The economics of storytelling are simple. It is a very inexpensive way to greatly improve your company in profound ways.
